prescan.instruction: Before analyzing any individual element, perceive the page as a whole composition of mathematical thinking:
prescan.step: View the entire page without reading. What is the overall spatial organization? (Single worked example? Multiple problems? Definition-then-examples? Two-column comparison?)
Identify the dominant content types: Is this page primarily algebraic work, geometric figures, graphing, a mix? What ratio of text to figures to equations?
Map the color landscape: How many distinct ink colors are present? Which color occupies the most area? Do colors appear to carry consistent semantic roles across the page?
Locate visual anchors: boxed answers, labeled figures, section headers, page numbers. These are your orientation landmarks.
Trace the pedagogical flow: Where does instruction begin on this page? What is the intended reading/working order?
Identify figure-text pairings: Which figures are associated with which text blocks? Proximity and alignment reveal these relationships.

primary.guidance: Separate mathematical content layers from the physical page: - FIGURE (primary content): Handwritten equations, solution steps, geometric constructions, function graphs, annotations - GROUND (structural/physical): Ruled lines on paper, grid lines, page edges, margin boundaries, bleed-through from reverse side - CRITICAL for handwritten math: Pencil-drawn geometric figures are often lighter than ink-written solutions overlaid on them. The figure is ground for the labels/measurements written on it, but figure relative to the blank page. - Multiple figure layers may exist: a pencil diagram is ground for blue ink annotations, which are ground for red boxed answers.

Spatial proximity defines mathematical semantic units: - Equations aligned vertically with consistent left margins form a SOLUTION CHAIN — they are sequential steps in a single derivation - Text immediately above or beside a figure is its LABEL or DESCRIPTION - An expression inside a box/circle is a FINAL ANSWER — the box boundary creates a proximity group - Side-by-side solution columns (e.g., solving for two unknowns simultaneously) are PARALLEL SOLUTION CHAINS - Annotations written between solution steps explain the OPERATION performed - Content separated by a horizontal divider line (wavy, straight, or decorative) indicates a TOPIC BREAK Key insight: In handwritten math, vertical alignment is the primary grouping axis for solution chains, while horizontal alignment groups parallel or comparative work.

Follow the logical flow of mathematical reasoning: - Solution chains flow top-to-bottom: each line should be derivable from the line above - Arrows or "therefore" symbols (∴) indicate explicit logical continuation - When a variable is isolated step-by-step (e.g., x+2=5 → x=3), continuity connects these as one derivation - Function transformation sequences (shift → reflect → shift) follow a defined order - In geometric proofs, the chain of angle/side correspondences follows a logical path - BREAK in continuity signals: horizontal divider lines, new "Example" labels, change in variables used, or significant spatial gaps

secondary.guidance: Visual consistency encodes mathematical meaning: - Same ink color throughout a solution chain = same writer/phase of work - Consistent use of a color for ALL section headers = organizational color coding - Same tick-mark pattern on triangle sides = EQUAL LENGTHS (congruence mark) - Same arc-mark pattern on angles = EQUAL ANGLE MEASURES - Repeated proportion structure (a/b = c/d pattern) = SIMILAR FIGURE reasoning - Same handwriting style throughout = single author; style change may indicate instructor vs. student

Recognize implied boundaries in mathematical content: - A box drawn around an expression (even if not fully closed) marks it as a FINAL ANSWER - A vinculum (overbar) in a radical sign √ groups everything under it as the radicand - Fraction bars create implicit grouping of numerator and denominator - Parentheses and brackets create expression boundaries even when handwriting is ambiguous about nesting - A geometric figure's shape is perceived as closed even if hand-drawn lines don't perfectly meet at vertices - Coordinate axes create four implicit quadrant regions

Shared spatial regions indicate related content: - All content within a single coordinate plane belongs to the same graphing context - All labels written inside or adjacent to a geometric figure describe that figure's properties - Content to the left of an equals sign and content to the right form a balanced pair - Side-by-side figures (e.g., two similar triangles drawn next to each other) are meant to be COMPARED - A "Let" or "Given" statement and the work below it share a problem context

pattern.name: vertical_derivation
pattern.description: The most common layout: equations stacked vertically, each derived from the one above. Left-aligned or center-aligned.
pattern.reading_order: Top to bottom. Each line is one logical step.
pattern.example_context: Solving equations, simplifying expressions, finding inverses.

pattern.name: two_column_solution
pattern.description: Two parallel solution chains side by side, often solving for different unknowns from the same setup.
pattern.reading_order: Left column top-to-bottom, then right column top-to-bottom (or interleaved if cross-referenced).
pattern.example_context: Finding QS and QR from similar triangles, solving systems of equations.

pattern.name: figure_with_adjacent_work
pattern.description: A geometric figure (triangle, graph, coordinate plane) with solution work written beside or below it.
pattern.reading_order: Figure provides visual context; adjacent text provides the algebraic work. Read together.

pattern.name: definition_then_example
pattern.description: A concept or property stated first, followed by one or more worked examples applying it.
pattern.reading_order: Definition/property first, then each example in sequence.
pattern.example_context: Section introductions: state theorem, then show application.

pattern.name: transformation_sequence
pattern.description: A base function followed by a series of transformations, often with before/after graphs.
pattern.reading_order: Base function → transformation 1 → transformation 2 → final form, possibly with graphs at each stage.
pattern.example_context: Graphing f(x) = -(x-1)² + 2 by starting from x² and applying shifts/reflections.

pattern.name: correspondence_mapping
pattern.description: Explicit pairing of elements between two structures (e.g., matching angles and sides of similar triangles).
pattern.reading_order: Left structure ↔ Right structure, with correspondence arrows or alignment.
pattern.example_context: △ABE ~ △CDE with angle and side correspondences listed.

geometric_figure_perception.instruction: When perceiving geometric figures, apply these specific strategies:
geometric_figure_perception.step: Identify the figure type: triangle, coordinate plane, circle, parallel lines with transversal, etc.
Locate ALL vertex labels — they are typically single uppercase letters placed at or near each vertex.
Read ALL numerical labels: side lengths (numbers on or near edges), angle measures (numbers with ° near vertices), and coordinates (ordered pairs near points).
Detect congruence marks: tick marks on sides indicate equal lengths; arc marks on angles indicate equal measures. COUNT the marks — single tick ≠ double tick ≠ triple tick.
Identify special marks: parallel arrows (→→) on sides, right angle squares (□) at 90° angles, midpoint marks.
Determine if the figure is drawn to scale or is schematic (most handwritten math figures are schematic).
Note any dashed or dotted lines — these typically indicate altitudes, medians, extensions, or construction lines.

color_perception.instruction: Systematically catalog ink colors and their semantic roles:
color_perception.step: Identify all distinct ink colors/media present on the page (pencil, blue ink, red ink, green ink, etc.).
For each color, note what TYPE of content it carries (figures? solutions? headers? answers?).
Look for consistent patterns: Is one color ALWAYS used for section headers? Is another ALWAYS for boxed answers?
Note cases where color changes MID-SOLUTION — this often indicates a switch between problem setup and solution work, or between different work sessions.
Record the color mapping as part of your analysis — downstream consumers need this to understand the page's semantic structure.

validation.instruction: After initial perception, verify your gestalt reading:
validation.check: Does your identified reading order match the logical flow of mathematical reasoning? (Each step should follow from the previous.)
Are all geometric figure labels accounted for? (Count vertices, count side labels, count angle labels.)
Do your proximity-based groupings make mathematical sense? (A solution chain shouldn't mix steps from different problems.)
Are color roles consistent across the page, or did you misidentify a color's function?
Have you correctly identified which content is a PROBLEM STATEMENT vs. SOLUTION WORK? (These have different downstream roles.)
Are figure-text pairings correct? (Does the algebraic work reference the variables/labels visible in the adjacent figure?)
